title: Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising

narrative.executive_view: Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising is an asset-heavy advertising business, not an ad agency: Census defines it as creating display campaigns and/or placing displays on billboards, panels, transit, malls and other structures [S1]. Its durable feature is permitted, leased and built physical inventory. OOH revenue reached $9.46 billion in 2025, grew 3.6%, and digital OOH accounted for 36.3% of revenue [S2]. AI can materially lower sales-support, design and administrative cost but cannot replace structures, permits, landowner leases or field maintenance. I classify terminal demand as DURABLE, while retaining low overall confidence because NAICS 541850 spans billboard plants, transit, indoor, display-design and other formats with very different economics.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The supplied 5418 role mix is agency-weighted, so it overstates PR, research and creative labour and omits much of the installation, printing, lease and field-operation tail. I use it only as the deterministic wage-share shell and judge actual tasks within it. AI can automate prospect lists, rate-card queries, proposal generation, proof-of-performance reports, billing, contract administration and creative iteration. Physical posting, structure maintenance, site permitting, lease negotiation and local selling remain partly or wholly human. BTOS’s 17–20% broad-business AI-use range shows adoption is early across firms, not already at a 50% effective-workflow threshold [S3].
narrative.value_capture: A display operator sells scarce inventory rather than hours. Billboard cash-flow guidance defines the asset cash flow after direct costs such as leases, maintenance, utilities, permit fees and an imputed sales cost [S4]. This supports a high, though not perfect, share of labour savings remaining with the operator: site rents are set by location and demand, not directly by back-office labour. Programmatic buying adds price transparency and a platform relationship, but OAAA data still show a mixed market: programmatic was 24% of DOOH spend in 2024 and outdoor inventory was 51% of spend on the Place Exchange platform in H1 2025 [S5]. The platform channel is a capture risk, not an assumption that it destroys the physical asset.
narrative.adoption_timing: I estimate current effective AI adoption at 25% and time to 50% at four years. BTOS provides a broad lower bound rather than a code-specific measurement [S3]. Adoption should be faster in creative, sales-support and billing teams, but the benefit is limited by smaller operators, disparate CMS and traffic systems, and the fact that most wage cost in a real plant includes field work that AI does not automate. Programmatic OOH is a related automation path, but it is a transaction technology, not proof that the whole operator has adopted AI [S5].
narrative.consolidation_economics: There is real, current buyer activity. Lamar completed more than 50 outdoor-asset acquisitions for $191.1 million in 2025 and acquired Verde Outdoor for $147.6 million; the filing identifies site locations, structures, contracts and leases as acquired assets [S6]. That supports one documented active consolidator and a credible exit path for quality assets. The multiple benchmark must be handled carefully: Billboard Insider reports 10–12x standardized billboard cash flow for well-maintained plants with a reasonable remaining lease portfolio [S4], while its Clear Channel analysis discusses 12.5–14x billboard cash flow [S7]. Those are cash-flow, not EBITDA, measures. I use 12.0x buy and 13.0x exit as transparent EBITDA proxies with a wide range, not as claimed observed EBITDA comps.
narrative.terminal_demand: The terminal is durable because AI cannot replicate a permitted display location, although media demand can still cycle and formats can lose share. OOH revenue grew for a nineteenth consecutive quarter through 2025, while traditional formats also posted gains and DOOH increased 10.5% [S2]. Lamar’s 2025 acquisitions and its allocation of consideration to site locations and advertising structures reinforce that scarcity has continuing economic value [S6]. A target with short leases, weak permits, poor locations or transit-management contracts is not protected by the industry result; terminal durability must be verified asset by asset.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The critical diligence risks are lease duration, permit status, structure condition, local demand, customer concentration, digital conversion capital and which portion of revenue is a physical plant rather than design or transit management. The deterministic target count uses a generic services margin and may undercount mature billboard plants; it is not changed here. The public multiple sources use standardized billboard cash flow rather than EBITDA, and OAAA statistics cover the full OOH medium including large public operators [S2, S4]. Owner age and succession evidence for independents is weak in reopened public sources, so those inputs remain low-confidence estimates.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System — 541850 Outdoor Advertising
sources.what_it_supports: Official scope: display campaign creation and/or display placement on indoor or outdoor billboards, panels, transit, malls and other structures.

sources.title: Out of Home Advertising Revenue Reaches Record $9.46 Billion
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 U.S. OOH revenue, year-over-year growth, 19 consecutive quarters of growth, and DOOH revenue share and growth.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.what_it_supports: BTOS broad-business AI use of 17–20% from December 2025 to May 2026 and lower reported use among the smallest firms.

sources.title: What’s Billboard Cashflow?
sources.what_it_supports: Billboard cash-flow definition, direct cost components, and 10–12x sale-multiple guidance for a well-maintained plant with a reasonable lease portfolio.

sources.title: Programmatic OOH Shows Continued Momentum in H1 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Programmatic OOH transaction trends, including outdoor’s share of Place Exchange platform spend, screen growth and deal mix; platform-specific rather than industry-wide.

sources.title: Lamar Advertising Company 2025 Form 10-K
sources.what_it_supports: Lamar’s 2025 outdoor-asset acquisitions, Verde Outdoor acquisition, and purchase-price allocation to site locations, structures, contracts and leases.

sources.title: Does a US Asset Sale Make Sense for Clear Channel?
sources.what_it_supports: Trade-source analysis of 12.5–14x billboard-cash-flow valuation scenarios and an historic Lamar asset-sale reference; not an EBITDA multiple.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI can automate sales-support, creative, scheduling, reporting and administration, but not the physical operating and site-control work that a true display operator carries. The supplied agency-weighted proxy requires a low-confidence reconstruction.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit 5418 workforce omits much installation, print, maintenance, permitting and lease work while overstating agency/PR roles.
Programmatic transaction growth is not counted as a V input; it is addressed in pi_dist and terminal discussion.

role_judgments.rationale: Prospecting, proposals, quoting and availability lookups automate; local selling remains.
Communication and reporting work is AI-assistable, but this is agency-proxy contamination.
Audience packaging, reporting and research are highly assistable.
Reporting automates but lease, permit and operational leadership persist.
Creative layouts and copy iteration are highly automatable.
Promotional support is partly automatable but not core plant labour.
Sales administration automates while advertiser and platform negotiation remains.
Physical display and trim work is largely not automated by current AI.
Planning and analysis automate; pricing and commercial leadership persist.
Creative direction is assisted but client judgment remains.
Residual is assumed to include physical field, print, lease and admin work; physical activity limits automation.

pi_dist.rationale: Location rental economics and direct-cost cash-flow structure support savings retention, while programmatic platforms increase price transparency and transaction intermediation.
caveats: No reopened source reports the platform take rate or AI savings pass-through for independent 541850 operators.

pi_moat.rationale: Permitted locations, remaining lease terms, structures and advertiser relationships create a physical-asset moat; Lamar’s acquired site-location and structure assets corroborate their economic importance.
caveats: Moat is asset-specific: short leases, non-transferable permits and poor locations can sharply reduce it.

t50_years.rationale: Current broad AI use remains below 50%; office and creative workflows can move quickly, but field-heavy operations and fragmented operators slow effective adoption.
caveats: Neither source is a 541850 workflow-adoption survey.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: A 25% effective-adoption estimate modestly exceeds broad BTOS use because programmatic workflow penetration is visible, without treating programmatic buying as full firm AI adoption.
caveats: BTOS is all-business evidence and Place Exchange is a selected platform, not the full code.

historical_analogs.value: Programmatic OOH’s growing transaction infrastructure is a relevant analogue for automated selling and reporting, while the physical site asset remains outside the analogue.
historical_analogs.rationale: OAAA/Place Exchange documents platform transaction, inventory and deal-mix momentum rather than an AI-specific adoption curve.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Automation may spread quickly in DOOH but remain limited in field operations and static plants.
caveats: The source covers one platform’s patterns and must not be read as all-industry adoption.

selection.rationale: A 45% working estimate recognizes small operator ownership but is not reported by the reopened source.
caveats: S4 discusses plant valuation and owner-operated sales expense, not a representative owner-age survey.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documents a code-level succession shortage, so this is conservatively false.
caveats: Not documented is not evidence of no target-level succession need.

active_consolidators.rationale: Lamar is one directly documented active consolidator: its 2025 10-K reports more than 50 outdoor-asset acquisitions plus Verde Outdoor.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: At least 1; complete private buyer count not established
caveats: Count is deliberately limited to an active acquirer documented in a reopened primary filing, not a census of all buyers.

buy_mult.rationale: Selected from reopened 10–12x and 12.5–14x standardized billboard-cash-flow discussions, using 12x as a conservative normalized-EBITDA proxy for a good lease-backed plant.
caveats: S4 and S7 report billboard cash flow, not EBITDA; accounting definitions, lease treatment and sales-cost normalization can make the proxy materially wrong.

exit_mult.rationale: A 13x EBITDA proxy assumes a buyer values a de-risked, lease-backed portfolio and realizes scale benefits; current Lamar acquisition activity supports an exit channel but not this exact multiple.
caveats: The source multiple is billboard cash flow, not EBITDA. Exit premium is not used in capture or terminal scoring.

pricing_structure.value: Advertising-rental revenue from display inventory, less direct plant costs including leases, permits, maintenance, utilities and sales cost
pricing_structure.rationale: The reopened industry source defines standardized billboard cash flow using advertising rental revenue and the listed direct costs.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Pricing and lease burden vary materially by format, geography and asset quality
caveats: This describes billboard plants; 541850 also includes indoor, transit and display-design activities.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A roughly 34% task-share estimate creates modest available uplift, while a four-year adoption estimate reflects that the most automatable work is not the whole physical operating model.

terminal_value.justification: Permitted, leased physical display inventory remains a paid asset while the full OOH market continued to grow; durability remains asset-specific.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The 4-digit 5418 role mix, BTOS adoption data, full-OOH revenue statistics and billboard-cash-flow multiples are all disclosed proxies or broader-than-code evidence.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Location scarcity supports savings retention, but programmatic buying can increase rate transparency and platform intermediation; this pressure is represented in pi_dist rather than terminal classification.

dataset_mismatch.description: The deterministic role mix is 4-digit 5418 and agency-weighted; it understates real display-operator field, installation, print, lease and permit work.
sensitivity: A larger physical-field share lowers V.
A larger design/sales-support share raises V.
The generic services margin may understate the number of mature billboard plants in the target EBITDA band.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Billboard/display-plant economics are used for capture and multiples because they are the only reopened asset-specific evidence; they do not represent every 541850 establishment.
market_boundary.selection_basis: Census scope and source availability; a target must be classified by its actual mix before applying the thesis.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 5.0 at four years to 3.3–5.0 across the stated interval.
V changes materially with the true physical-field labour share absent from the proxy.
M is sensitive to the unobserved conversion from standardized billboard cash flow to EBITDA and to lease quality.

reviewer_note: All seven score-driving source URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. The multiple sources report standardized billboard cash flow rather than EBITDA; packet values therefore state an explicit proxy conversion caveat instead of asserting an observed EBITDA multiple.
